answer:
The corpus surfaces one clear Iqbal passage and one Ghalib passage that engage the alast (pre-eternal covenant) theme.

**Iqbal — Bang-e-Dara Part 1, ch.37 "Sarguzasht-e-Adam" (The Saga of Man)**
The opening couplet is the key passage: "Come, hear from me the story of my exile's way; / The tale of the First Covenant I let fall away." (Urdu transliteration: "bhulaya / qissa-e-paimaan-e-awwaleen mein ne"). This is an explicit invocation of the alast pact — paimaan-e-awwaleen, the primordial covenant. But Iqbal immediately reframes it: the poem's voice (Adam/humanity) says he deliberately let the covenant fall away, because his spirit found no rest in Paradise once he drank "the fiery cup of consciousness." The remainder of the poem catalogues centuries of restless, willful striving — expelling idols from the Ka'ba, climbing Sinai, dying on the Cross, meditating in Hira's cave, carrying truth to Greece, India, China — before finally finding God dwelling within one's own heart. The alast is not a moment of passive assent but the origin-point of an active, self-willed quest across history. Breaking the covenant was itself a form of striving.

**Ghalib — Diwan-e-Ghalib, ch.153 "Naqsh faryadi hai kis ki shokhi-e-tehreer ka"**
The opening couplet is the key passage: "Whose playful script does this whole painted world complain of? / Each figure wears the paper robes a petitioner is made of." (Urdu: "Naqsh faryadi hai kis ki shokhi-e-tehreer ka / Kaaghazi hai pairahan har paikar-e-tasveer ka"). This is the alast tradition rendered as cosmological complaint: the created world (naqsh, the painted figure) is a faryadi — a supplicant filing a petition against the very script God wrote at creation. Every being is dressed in paper (kaaghazi pairahan), the garb of a petitioner before a court. The tone is elegiac and resigned: existence is a decree already written in divine playfulness (shokhi-e-tehreer), and all creatures can do is complain of it. Ghalib offers no escape from this scripted fate; the poem accepts entrapment as the irreducible condition of being.

**How Iqbal reframes Ghalib's resignation into action**
Ghalib treats the pre-eternal inscription as a finality that traps the self — we are paper figures, faryadi, helplessly petitioning against a fate already fixed. The tone is one of beautiful, melancholic acceptance. Iqbal takes the same metaphysical premise — that a covenant was struck before creation — and inverts its valence. In "Sarguzasht-e-Adam," breaking or "forgetting" the first covenant is not a fall but a choice: Adam drained the fiery cup of consciousness and chose restless striving over the stasis of Paradise. The alast becomes not a scripted cage but a point of departure. Where Ghalib's petitioner is passive (wearing paper robes, filing complaints), Iqbal's Adam is the agent of his own exile — he bhulaya (caused the forgetting), he chose the fiery cup, he drove civilisation forward across continents. The pre-eternal pact is transformed from a decree to be lamented into the very energy that impels khudi (the self) toward its unfolding. Resignation becomes action; petition becomes quest.
